perturbation.original_text: Within 7 Business Days after the end of each calendar quarter, the Billing Party shall provide the other party with an itemized statement, subject to adjustment within 30 days of receipt of such statement, setting forth the Net Revenue achieved over the preceding calendar quarter, and shall include such other information as may be mutually agreed upon by the parties (the "Revenue Report").
perturbation.changed_text: Within 60 Business Days after the end of each calendar quarter, the Billing Party shall provide the other party with an itemized statement, subject to adjustment within 90 days of receipt of such statement, setting forth the Net Revenue achieved over the preceding calendar quarter, and shall include such other information as may be mutually agreed upon by the parties (the "Revenue Report").
perturbation.explanation: Changing the reporting and adjustment periods significantly delays financial transparency, potentially violating regulations requiring timely and accurate financial reporting. A 60-day reporting window and a 90-day adjustment window are longer than what is typically considered reasonable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act mandates that public companies establish and maintain internal controls to ensure accurate and reliable financial reporting. Extending the reporting and adjustment periods to 60 and 90 days respectively significantly increases the risk of inaccuracies and delays in financial reporting, potentially violating Section 404 of SOX (15 U.S. Code § 7262) concerning management assessment of internal controls.

perturbation.justification: The change from 7 business days to 60 business days for reporting, and 30 to 90 days for adjustment, directly contradicts the intent of timely and accurate financial reporting as emphasized by the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. The Law Explanation highlights that extending these periods increases the risk of inaccuracies and delays. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 7241, mandates regulations for companies filing periodic reports, requiring principal officers to certify these reports. While the snippet doesn't specify exact timelines, the original contract's timeframe aligns better with the spirit of accurate and timely reporting than the significantly extended timeframe. The changed text introduces a significant delay that makes it more difficult to quickly identify and correct inaccuracies, going against the corporate responsibility for financial reports. Therefore, the modification introduces inconsistency that creates a legal contradiction, rendering the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The Billing Party shall, for two years following each transaction relating to the Co-Branded Service, keep true and accurate records and books of account of such transaction containing all particulars which may be necessary for the purpose of auditing payments to the Non-Billing Party under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Billing Party shall, for one year following each transaction relating to the Co-Branded Service, keep true and accurate records and books of account of such transaction containing all particulars which may be necessary for the purpose of auditing payments to the Non-Billing Party under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record-keeping period to one year may conflict with accounting regulations that require longer retention periods for financial records. This hinders the ability to conduct thorough audits and verify financial transactions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 17a-4 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 requires broker-dealers to preserve records for prescribed time periods, often longer than one year, depending on the record type. Reducing the retention period to one year violates this rule by limiting the availability of records for regulatory audits and investigations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.17a-4 Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. This section applies to the following types of entities: A member of a national securities exchange who transacts a business in securities directly with others than members of a national securities exchange ; a broker or dealer who transacts a business in securities through the medium of a member of a national securities exchange ; a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer as that term is defined in § 240.3b-12 , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 o ); a security-based swap dealer registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 o -10) that is also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act; and a major security-based swap participant registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act. Section 240.18a-6 (rather than this section) applies to the following types of entities: A security-based swap dealer registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is not also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act; and a major security-based swap participant registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is not also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act. (a) Every member , broker or dealer subject to § 240.17a-3 must preserve for a period of not less than 6 years, the first two years
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the record-keeping period from two years to one year, which directly contradicts Rule 17a-4 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 240.17a-4, states that members, brokers, and dealers must preserve records for a period of not less than 6 years. The modified contract, by only requiring one year, creates a clear non-compliance with the law.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time in the event that the other party is in default or breach of any material provision of this Agreement, and such default or breach continues unremedied for a period of sixty days after written notice thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time in the event that the other party is in default or breach of any material provision of this Agreement, and such default or breach continues unremedied for a period of ten days after written notice thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to ten days may be insufficient under contract law principles of reasonableness and good faith, particularly for complex breaches. This drastically short timeline may impede on their ability to take corrective action. Many jurisdictions allow for a reasonable cure period, which is almost always longer than 10 days.
perturbation.law_explanation: While NY UCC § 2-609 deals with adequate assurance of performance, it reflects a broader principle that parties are entitled to a reasonable opportunity to cure breaches. Reducing the cure period to ten days may be deemed commercially unreasonable, particularly if the breach involves complex issues or requires significant corrective action, potentially conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under New York law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 6 Breach, Repudiation and Excuse previous SECTION 2-608 Revocation of Acceptance in Whole or in Part up PART 6 Breach, Repudiation and Excuse next SECTION 2-610 Anticipatory Repudiation This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-609 Right to Adequate Assurance of Performance Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 6 Section 2--609. Right to Adequate Assurance of Performance. (1) A contract for sale imposes an obligation on each party that the other's expectation of receiving due performance will not be impaired. When reasonable grounds for insecurity arise with respect to the performance of either party the other may in writing demand adequate assurance of due performance and until he receives such assurance may if commercially reasonable suspend any performance for which he has not already received the agreed return. (2) Between merchants the reasonableness of grounds for insecurity and the adequacy of any assurance offered shall be determined according to commercial standards. (3) Acceptance of any improper delivery or payment does not prejudice the aggrieved party's right to demand adequate assurance of future performance. (4) After receipt of a justified demand failure to provide within a reasonable time not exceeding thirty days such assurance of due performance as is adequate under the circumstances of the particular case is a repudiation of the contract.
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the cure period from sixty days to ten days. The Law Explanation argues that this may be deemed commercially unreasonable, especially for complex breaches. Scraped Law Snippet 1, NY UCC § 2-609, addresses the right to adequate assurance of performance and states that failure to provide assurance within a reasonable time not exceeding thirty days can be a repudiation of the contract. While the snippet focuses on assurance, the Law Explanation extends the principle to curing breaches, and by reducing the cure period to 10 days, the contract risks conflicting with principles of reasonableness and good faith. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that this short timeline may be insufficient for corrective action, thereby potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which is a form of legal contradiction as defined.
